planting.note: Plant corms 3 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic to neutral soil in full sun; baboon flower grows through the cool season and blooms in spring, then goes dormant in summer. In zone 8–10 with dry summers, the corms can be left in the ground permanently. In wetter climates, lift and store dry through summer. The common name comes from baboons in South Africa digging and eating the corms.

directions: Plant corms in fall in very well-drained, sandy soil in full sun; baboon flower is a charming South African spring bulb from the Cape Floristic Region, producing pleated, fan-like foliage and dense spikes of bright, vivid flowers in a wide range of colors — violet, purple, blue, pink, red, cream, and white — in late winter to spring. The flowers are fragrant in many varieties, with a light, sweet scent. The common name derives from baboons digging and eating the corms in their native South Africa (hence 'babiana' from 'baboon'). Growing on a Mediterranean bulb cycle — fall planting, cool season growth, spring bloom, summer dormancy — it naturalizes well in California, the Gulf Coast, and similar mild, dry-summer climates. The dense, ribbed, pleated leaves are distinctive. Different color forms are available as mixed-color bulb packages or separately. Lovely for late winter and early spring color when few bulbs are blooming, combining beautifully with freesias, ixia, and sparaxis from the same season.

subcategory: South African long-lasting cut flower bulb

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in very well-drained, sandy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; chinkerinchee grows through the cool season and blooms in late spring. In zone 9–10 it naturalizes; in cooler zones, lift after the foliage dies and store dry through summer. It is widely grown as a commercial cut flower crop in South Africa and exported worldwide for its exceptional vase life.

directions: Plant bulbs in fall in very well-drained, sandy soil in full sun; chinkerinchee is a South African Cape bulb famous in the cut flower trade for the extraordinary vase life of its flowers — blooms can last 3–4 weeks in a vase, making it one of the longest-lasting cut flowers available. The dense, conical spikes of pure-white, star-shaped flowers with dark centers are produced in late spring on upright, 18–24 inch stems. The flowers open progressively from bottom to top over several weeks, extending the display period. The vernacular name 'chinkerinchee' comes from the sound the dry stem makes when rubbed against itself in a breeze. It is a major South African agricultural export, with bulbs wrapped in newspaper and shipped around the world because of their unusual longevity out of water during transport. The flowers are also attractive in the garden or naturalized in zone 9–10, forming colonies where the dry summer dormancy can be accommodated.

planting.note: Plant offsets or container-grown plants in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; coral aloe is the most cold-tolerant flat-leafed aloe and tolerates brief freezes to 25°F. It requires less water than most garden plants but is killed by prolonged wet soil. One of the finest aloes for mild-climate gardens as it is spineless (safe for children), ornamental, and produces outstanding hummingbird-attracting flowers.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, lean, alkaline to neutral soil in full sun; coral aloe is widely considered one of the finest landscape aloes for mild-climate gardens — a flat-growing, rosette-forming South African species completely lacking the teeth and spines that make most aloes difficult to handle. The broad, smooth, glaucous blue-gray to gray-green leaves with thin, pinkish margins are attractive all year, taking on warm peach-apricot tones in cool weather. In winter to spring, dramatic branched flower spikes arise carrying clusters of vivid coral-red to orange tubular flowers that attract hummingbirds in numbers throughout the winter and early spring, providing a critical nectar source when little else is in bloom. Coral aloe spreads slowly by offsets to form impressive colonies and is an outstanding, child-safe alternative to spiny aloes in gardens frequented by children. Its lack of spines, glaucous foliage, spectacular winter flowers, and hummingbird attraction make it arguably the most garden-worthy of the commonly available aloes.

planting.note: Plant container-grown plants in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, rocky or gravelly soil in full sun; Thompson's yucca is more compact and smaller than Y. rostrata but is sometimes confused with it. It is native to the limestone mountain ranges of the Big Bend region of Texas and northern Mexico and is adapted to the same extreme drought and alkaline limestone soils.

directions: Plant in very well-drained, alkaline to neutral, lean, rocky soil in full sun; Thompson's yucca is a blue-gray-leafed native of the Trans-Pecos limestone mountains of west Texas — one of the most ornamental small to medium tree yuccas, developing a slowly lengthening trunk over many years and producing a symmetrical, rounded head of narrow, blue-gray, finely toothed leaves. Its blue-gray foliage is similar to Yucca rostrata but the plant stays somewhat more compact and typically doesn't develop as tall a trunk. The narrow, glaucous leaves create a fine-textured, spherical crown that is outstanding as a landscape specimen and combines well with native grasses, agaves, and other desert-adapted plants. In spring, impressive, branched flower spikes 4–6 feet tall bear numerous large, white, bell-shaped flowers. Native to the Chihuahuan Desert and adjacent ranges in Texas and Mexico, it is excellent for dry, alkaline, rocky landscape situations in zones 6–10.

planting.note: Direct-sow in fall for biennial flowering in spring of the second year, or sow in very early spring; woad is a biennial or short-lived perennial in the mustard family. INVASIVE WARNING: Woad is a listed noxious weed in multiple western US states (Idaho, Colorado, Utah, Montana, Nevada, Oregon, and others) and should not be planted in those states. Check local regulations before planting.

directions: Sow in well-drained, average soil in full sun — INVASIVE in western US states: check local regulations; woad is the ancient European source of blue dye that predates the introduction of tropical indigo (Indigofera tinctoria) — the same compound, indigotin, is present in both plants, but in smaller concentrations in woad. The blue woad dye produced from fermented, composted leaves was the primary blue textile dye of Europe for thousands of years, from prehistoric times through the Middle Ages, and was used to produce the blue in medieval stained glass pigments, illuminated manuscripts, and textiles. The Picts of Scotland famously used woad to paint their bodies blue. The plant itself is an upright biennial producing rosettes of blue-green basal leaves in the first year and tall branching flower stalks bearing masses of small, bright-yellow flowers in the second year, followed by distinctive, hanging, black, paddle-shaped seed pods that are ornamental. It was supplanted by imported indigo in the 16th–17th centuries. Now grown primarily as a historical, educational, and natural dye plant.
sources: Dye Plants and Dyeing (Brooklyn Botanic Garden)
